text: The surgical team initiates the Hartman procedure for acute diverticulitis in a sixty-year-old obese woman. Upon induction of pneumoperitoneum, the absence of adhesions is noted, along with the presence of an incarcerated peristomal hernia. The surgeon begins the reduction of the peristomal hernia.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the splenic flexure, identifying a suitable plane in the virgin field. The inferior mesenteric vein and left colic artery are cut, while the inferior mesenteric artery and ureter are not isolated.
The surgeon completes the lateral to medial mobilization of the descending colon, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon detaches the stoma and suspends the pneumoperitoneum. The head of the circular stapler is inserted into the colon for anastomosis. The pelvis is free from adhesions, and the rectal wall is exposed using the circular stapler handpiece. The vagina is identified and retracted. The operation concludes with a mechanical circular anastomosis.
